ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried thick rice vermicelli with seafood; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP for stir-fried noodles used as closest parent; no specific code for thick bee hoon with seafood exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific stir-fried noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, equivalent to a single plate of stir-fried thick bee hoon with seafood as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Bee Hoon with Seafood — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists fried bee hoon with seafood as a standard hawker dish. Values for thick bee hoon variant are adapted from the HPB fried bee hoon with seafood entry and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data for individual seafood and rice noodle components to confirm plausibility. Thick bee hoon has a slightly lower energy density than thin bee hoon due to higher water retention; values adjusted accordingly. Per-100g figures are derived from a representative 350 g serving composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice noodles and mixed stir-fried noodle dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried thick bee hoon with seafood. GI estimated at 53 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (GI ~53, low category) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle. The addition of protein-rich seafood, egg, and vegetables further attenuates the glycemic response, supporting a GI at or below 53. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 34.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 18.5 × 3.5 = 64.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per serving) from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a single 350 g serving provides approximately 64% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (~64.75 g per serving) from thick rice vermicelli, partially offset by low GI (~53) and protein from seafood
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish in controlled portions; the low GI of rice noodles and the protein content of seafood moderate postprandial glucose rise, but the total glycemic load per serving (~34) remains significant and should be accounted for in daily carbohydrate budgeting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium per serving and moderate cholesterol (~68 mg per 100 g) from seafood and egg, partially offset by low saturated fat content (~1.1 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the high sodium load per serving and moderate dietary cholesterol from prawns and squid; choosing smaller portions and requesting less sauce can help align intake with heart-healthy dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Thick Bee Hoon with Seafood (海鲜炒粗米粉) is a popular Singaporean hawker and restaurant dish featuring thick rice vermicelli noodles (粗米粉, chor bee hoon) wok-fried at high heat with a medley of fresh seafood — typically prawns, squid, and fish cake — along with egg, bean sprouts, spring onion, and garlic. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper, producing a savoury, slightly smoky flavour characteristic of wok hei. The thick bee hoon has a soft, chewy texture that absorbs the sauce well, distinguishing it from the thinner, more delicate thin bee hoon. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 497 kcal, 25 g protein, 17 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate. The dish is a moderate source of protein from seafood and egg, and contains notable sodium (~1470 mg per serving) primarily from soy and oyster sauces. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low), making it a relatively moderate glycemic choice among noodle dishes, though the glycemic load per serving is substantial (~34). Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the moderate carbohydrate load requires portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal when prepared with permissible seafood and sauces.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for thick bee hoon with seafood was identified; values are adapted from the HPB fried bee hoon with seafood category and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle entry. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adaptation rather than exact match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish category. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the standard preparation of this dish per HPB source data. The GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this specific dish was located. Sodium content is high due to soy sauce and oyster sauce; this is the primary clinical concern. The field dataed in the source (Viet'Spice) serves a Vietnamese-influenced variant; the nutritional values here represent the standard Singaporean hawker-style preparation. Price data (SGD 19.90) is preserved from the field survey source record for reference only and does not influence nutritional values.
